artifacts.content:
Mom,

Finally sitting down for a minute, so I figured I’d actually write instead of just shooting you a photo of some muddy horse.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how long Laura’s been in my corner. It’s wild that it’s been just over nine years since that spring of 2017 when I first hauled that goofy 5‑year‑old mustang mare to Henderson Ranch outside Loveland. Remember how Dad had to come with me and co‑sign Laura’s training contract because I’d maxed out my credit card and missed that truck payment that April? I was so embarrassed, but honestly that’s what kept me from losing the mare.

That whole season feels like a blur now—me trying to juggle CSU, driving back and forth to Fort Collins for classes, then out to Henderson Ranch to ride, and then everything blowing up with my ex in Greeley. I still don’t know what I would have done if Laura hadn’t let me crash on her couch for those six weeks that first summer when I didn’t feel safe going back to that apartment.

Fast‑forward to now and I’m the one people are calling about their wild horses, and I’ve somehow got my own 10 acres leased out here on County Road 17. Today was typical: fed at 6, worked four horses before lunch, then ran into town to Jax for hay cubes and fly spray. Dan’s on a deadline with that solar project in Fort Collins, so I’ll probably ride Ruger after dark under the arena lights again.

Anyway, just feeling weirdly grateful and nostalgic tonight. Tell Dad I still remember him sitting at Laura’s kitchen table reading every line of that contract like it was a house mortgage.

Love you,
Emily

metadata.subject: Thinking about 2017 and how far things have come

artifacts.content:
Personal journal – late night barn thoughts

I keep circling back to that decision in 2017 to walk away from CSU’s Equine Science program. I still remember calling Laura from the parking lot after another panic attack in Dr. Keller’s 8 a.m. anatomy lab in Fort Collins. It was May 2017, I’d already dropped one quiz because I couldn’t make myself walk into that room, and my chest felt like it was going to explode. Walking out of CSU felt like failing at the time, but if I hadn’t, I never would’ve started spending all those evenings at Henderson Ranch actually working horses instead of memorizing muscles.

Same year, same mess: breaking up with the Greeley boyfriend, sleeping on Laura’s couch for about six weeks that first summer, trying to figure out if I was completely screwing up my life. And yet, here I am now with my own place on County Road 17, a string of mustangs in the 50' round pen, and clients who trust me with their “problem” horses.

I need to remember that when the old anxiety creeps back—like it did after Dad’s radiation at UCHealth Cancer Center last March, or when I was driving Jake to those 6 a.m. DUI classes at the Larimer County Justice Center and then trying to be cheerful for 4‑H kids by 9. My path has never been tidy, but it’s mine, and the horses don’t care that I never finished the B.S. They care that I show up, read their feet and their eyes, and don’t quit on them.

Note to self: that’s enough.

metadata.title: Journal – why leaving CSU still haunts me (and why I’d do it again)

artifacts.content:
Hey Laura,

Long overdue update from the frozen north (aka Wellington).

First, facilities: I’m still really glad we did that walkthrough with the Larimer County zoning inspector back in July 2021 after the neighbor’s “dangerous mustangs” complaint. Him saying my 50' solid‑sided round pen, 24' x 24' starting pen, and no‑climb perimeter fence on the 10 acres out here on County Road 17 actually exceeded their standards has been in the back of my mind all winter. It made me a lot calmer when I brought that bay BLM mare back from your place after she blew through the cheaper panels last August and ended up needing stitches at CSU’s Johnson Family Equine Hospital. Six‑week rehab and restart here, zero fence drama.

Same story when Dan’s reactive bay gelding that went through two fences and caused that minor car accident on County Road 19 in March 2022 came here for 90 days—no escapes from the 6' solid round pen, even when the wind was howling.

On the people side, I’m trying not to repeat my November 2020 wash‑rack meltdown. I’ve kept up the every‑other‑Tuesday 3 p.m. sessions with Dr. Greene at Summit Counseling in Fort Collins, and it’s helping me say no. Case in point: I just turned down a Windsor client who wanted a 30‑day miracle on a bolty mare. Old me would’ve said yes and then ridden 10 horses a day until I cracked.

Anyway, if you’ve got any spring clinic dates at Henderson Ranch, send them my way. I’d love to haul in with Ruger once the arena isn’t a skating rink.

Hugs,
Emily

metadata.subject: Pens, panic, and planning for spring

artifacts.content:
Event: Evening support rides with Emily – post‑accident
Time: Recurring, Mon/Wed/Fri 5:30–9:00 p.m.
Location: Cartwright Horsemanship, 41725 County Road 17, Wellington
Attendees: Emily, Dan (sometimes), Laura (occasional)

Notes:

Started this routine after the trailer wreck on I‑25 near Loveland in October 2022. Even though the horses walked away and the insurance handled the trailer, Emily’s been having panic attacks if she rides alone after dark. From November 2022 through February 2023 I’ve been making sure either I or Dan is around 4–5 nights a week so she can still keep 2–3 horses going under the arena lights without white‑knuckling it.

Typical evening:
– 5:30 p.m.: I get to the barn, quick check of waters and pens (especially the smaller secure pens we used for Ruger’s 60‑day post‑colic‑surgery rehab after CSU Johnson sent him home in November 2022).
– 6:00–8:30 p.m.: Emily rides or does groundwork with 2–3 horses in the 50' round pen or outdoor, sometimes ponies one down the irrigation ditch trail behind the property. I stay in the arena or within shouting distance in case she gets lightheaded (thinking about that January 2024 anemia fainting spell in the barn aisle).
– 8:30–9:00 p.m.: Wrap up, grain, lights off.

Remind Emily to schedule her follow‑up with Dr. Patel at Banner Fort Collins Medical Center about the anemia, and to keep using the breathing exercises Dr. Greene gave her for the accident‑related anxiety.

metadata.title: Recurring – Evening support rides after I‑25 trailer wreck

artifacts.content:
Hey Jake,

Figured I’d give you the full download before Mom edits it.

Barn’s fine, horses are fine. The only real scare lately was in January when I straight‑up fainted in the barn aisle at Cartwright Horsemanship from the iron‑deficiency anemia flaring back up. Dan freaked, of course. Dr. Patel at Banner Fort Collins Medical Center has me on supplements and told me not to ride alone in the afternoons for a while, so Laura’s been swinging by 3–4 days a week from about 2–5 p.m. while I work 3–5 horses in the round pen and arena.

It feels a little like Spring 2021 all over again when I was hauling you to those 6 a.m. DUI classes at the Larimer County Justice Center in Fort Collins and then racing back here to feed by 7:30. Different crisis, same level of tired.

Money’s its usual circus. In January 2024 two client checks bounced, so I ended up behind on Ruger’s board at Henderson Ranch in Loveland for the month. I took in a nervous BLM gelding for a 60‑day TIP prep at the same time, and Laura gently asked if I was sure I wasn’t overloading myself just to plug the hole. I actually turned down another horse from a Windsor client, which felt weird but also kind of grown‑up.

On the plus side, the county zoning thing from July 2021 still gives me some peace of mind—knowing that inspector walked the whole 10 acres on County Road 17 and said my 50' solid round pen, 24x24 starting pen, and perimeter fencing exceeded their standards. At least when the wind is howling and the mustangs are snorting, I’m not also worrying about them ending up on the highway.

If you’re up this weekend, I’ve got a gate that could use your welding magic.

Love,
Em

metadata.subject: Health, horses, and a tiny bit of grown‑up budgeting

artifacts.content:
Laura,

I just need to vent to someone who understands both horses and spreadsheets.

September really kicked my butt. Karen Lewis from Windsor is now three full months behind on Cisco’s $850/month board and training. I know, I know, I should have drawn a harder line, but you’ve seen her situation. Sending that mustang home to a half‑finished setup and her bad back felt worse than carrying the balance.

So I set up an informal payment plan and kept Cisco in full work. To make it pencil out, I put about $2,500 on my personal Visa this fall between hay from Jax Farm & Ranch and that surprise $640 Four Pines Equine bill when he popped the eye ulcer. Ethically I couldn’t not treat it. Financially… oof.

It brought back all the old stuff from March 2023 when Dad was going through radiation at UCHealth Cancer Center in Fort Collins. I’d ride 5–6 client horses during the day here at Cartwright Horsemanship, then drive to Greeley Regional Hospital most evenings to sit with him until visiting hours ended. That’s when the panic attacks started ramping up again and I ended up back on the low‑dose anxiety meds from my primary care at Banner Health Clinic in Wellington.

I’m trying hard not to slide back into the November 2020 version of me who was riding up to 10 horses a day at Henderson Ranch, had the mini breakdown in your wash rack, and finally agreed to see Dr. Allison Greene at Summit Counseling every other Tuesday at 3 p.m. The therapy has helped me say “no,” but my heart still overrules my calculator when a horse’s welfare is on the line.

Anyway, if you have any wisdom on balancing compassion with staying solvent, I’m all ears.

Love,
Emily

metadata.subject: Cisco, credit cards, and trying not to burn out (again)

artifacts.content:
Client & crisis log – for my own sanity

Jan 2022 – 4‑H clinic at Larimer County Fairgrounds. One of “my” kids came off in the green‑horse group. Helmet did its job but she still ended up with a mild concussion. I rode in the ambulance with her to Poudre Valley Hospital ER in Fort Collins and stayed almost five hours with her and her parents, talking through what the accident report would say and how a claim under the club’s liability policy might work. Spent the next two weeks doing quick check‑in calls every few days until the doctor cleared her to ride. Didn’t tell the other parents much—her story to share, not mine.

May 2022 – Client mare colicked overnight at my Wellington place. Hauled to High Plains Equine Veterinary Services in Fort Collins at 3 a.m. Dr. Tran recommended emergency surgery (~$3,500). Owner was shaking, I signed as the one in charge of the horse. He was clear it was a bad‑luck medical event, not my feed or management, but I still cried in the truck afterward and half‑expected all her horses to leave my program.

March 2023 – Weird phone call from a Greeley client saying she’d reported me to Larimer County Animal Control over a 90‑day colt‑starting bill. Laura drove out to County Road 17 and checked the two geldings—good weight, fresh water, just seen by Dr. Tran for spring shots. Complaint went nowhere, but my hands shook for an hour.

Writing this down to remind myself: I handled each thing. Horses okay, kids okay, still here.

metadata.title: Log – incidents I keep replaying in my head
